title: Alice Veksler
text:
Faculty directory observation
Name: Alice Veksler
Snapshot: 2026-07-21
biography: Dr. Veksler's primary area of teaching and research expertise is in Public Health, and in particular, in Health Communication. Broadly, her work looks at the effects of various types of interpersonal interactions on stress. Her current lines of research investigate how feeling restricted in one's ability to talk about organizational stress (CROS), can lead to negative health outcomes such as burnout, for healthcare providers, and how not being able to rely on a support person affects adjustment to college. She is passionate about health education and hopes that her work helps improve health outcomes at individual and community levels. In addition to serving as the Director of the Health Studies program, Dr. Veksler also serves as Director of the Health Studies Biofluid Research Lab, and is a Professor in the Department of Communication and in Health Studies.
education_entries: MPH in Public Health, Old Dominion University
Ph D in Communication Studies, Arizona State University
MA in Communication Studies, San Diego State University
research_interests: Public Health, Health Communication, Interpersonal Communication, Stress, Biology of Communication, Relational Maintenance, Conflict, Persuasion & Social Influence, Human Physiology, and Measure & Scale Development.
teaching_interests: Public Health, Health & Medical Communication, Stress Health & Human Interaction, Conflict, Negotiation & Mediation, Personal Wellness, Quantitative Research Methods, Senior Seminar/Capstone, & Internship.
publications: Journal Article, Academic Journal Effects of Mood on Conversational Argument Acts and Sequences: A Minimally Rational Perspective . Argumentation.

Journal Article, Academic Journal (2023). The stress of nursing: exploring communicatively restricted organizational stress (CROS), effort-reward imbalance, and organizational support among a sample of U.S. working nurses . Journal of Occupational Medicine and Toxicology. Volume, 18. Issue, 1. Pages, 22.
